tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Two landmark U.S. Supreme Court decisions—one in the 1980s involving a home video recording device challenged by movie studios, and another in the 2010s involving a foreign student reselling lower-priced textbooks from abroad—both significantly strengthened the “first sale” doctrine. Identify these two cases and explain: (1) the core legal issue in each dispute, (2) how the Court ruled, and (3) how the reasoning in these decisions protected or reshaped the business model of video rental and resale companies such as major brick‑and‑mortar chains and later DVD-by-mail or kiosk services. In your answer, connect the legal principles to the evolution of the video rental shop industry.

The Supreme Court’s endorsement of the “first sale” doctrine in the 1980s helped enable the rise of video rental shops by protecting the right to rent or resell lawfully purchased tapes and discs. Decades later, several technology companies and a start-up attempted to apply similar resale principles to purely digital media, proposing systems that would allow consumers to transfer or resell legally acquired e-books or music files without creating duplicate copies. Identify the two major technology companies that sought patents for digital resale marketplaces, as well as the start-up that was sued by a major record label over its secondary market for digital songs. For each entity, describe (1) how its proposed or implemented system would prevent multiple copies from existing simultaneously, and (2) the specific legal or industry reaction it faced. Answer length: 250-350 words.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: Two landmark U.S. Supreme Court decisions—one in the 1980s involving a home video recording device challenged by movie studios, and another in the 2010s involving a foreign student reselling lower-priced textbooks from abroad—both significantly strengthened the “first sale” doctrine. Identify these two cases and explain: (1) the core legal issue in each dispute, (2) how the Court ruled, and (3) how the reasoning in these decisions protected or reshaped the business model of video rental and resale companies such as major brick‑and‑mortar chains and later DVD-by-mail or kiosk services. In your answer, connect the legal principles to the evolution of the video rental shop industry.

Description of the 5-4 Supreme Court ruling allowing the VCR to survive and recognizing lawful home time-shifting
Explanation that Congress refused to ban renting or reselling lawfully purchased videotapes for profit under first sale doctrine
Identification of video rental businesses (e.g., Blockbuster, Netflix, Redbox) protected by first sale rights
Explanation of the Kirtsaeng case’s core issue (resale of legally purchased foreign editions in the U.S.)
Description of the 6-3 Supreme Court ruling affirming that first sale applies to legally purchased goods made abroad
Connection between first sale doctrine and resale/rental markets, including implications for media distribution models

Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013) + identified as the case about importing and reselling lower-priced foreign textbooks under first sale doctrine

It uses wide aggregation by demanding details about both rulings and linking them to the development and protection of video rental shop businesses (Blockbuster, Netflix, Redbox) under the first sale doctrine.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The two cases are **Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (1984)** and **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013)**.

1. **Core legal issue (Sony)**: Movie studios claimed Sony’s Betamax VCR enabled copyright infringement and that Sony should be secondarily liable. The broader issue was whether home recording and later rental of lawfully purchased tapes violated copyright. [1]  
2. **Ruling (Sony)**: The Court held that private, noncommercial “time-shifting” was fair use and that a technology maker isn’t liable if the device is capable of substantial non‑infringing uses. [1] This effectively allowed widespread sale of VCRs and tapes, making the independent video rental store model lawful: once a store bought a tape, it could rent it out under the first sale doctrine without extra licenses. Studios were pushed toward selling, not only controlling exhibition.

3. **Core legal issue (Kirtsaeng)**: A student imported and resold cheaper foreign‑edition textbooks. The publisher argued the first sale doctrine didn’t apply to copyrighted works manufactured abroad. [2]  
4. **Ruling (Kirtsaeng)**: The Court held first sale applies regardless of where a copy is made, so lawful owners of foreign‑made copies can resell them in the U.S. [2] This strengthened secondary markets and confirmed that owners of legitimately purchased DVDs, including foreign-made discs, could rent or resell them—supporting business models of chains like Blockbuster, Netflix’s DVD‑by‑mail, and Redbox kiosks, which rely on buying copies once and then repeatedly renting them out without ongoing studio control.

Together, these decisions underpinned the legality and economics of the video rental and resale ecosystem, enabling its growth from local shops to national chains and mail/kiosk services.

## References
[1] Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984).  
[2] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013).
rounds.answer_b:
The two landmark Supreme Court decisions are *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.* (1984) and *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* (2013) [1][2].

In *Sony* (the "Betamax case"), the core issue was whether the sale of home video recording devices (VCRs) constituted contributory copyright infringement since consumers used them to record copyrighted television broadcasts [1]. The Court ruled in Sony's favor, concluding the devices were legal because they were capable of "substantial non-infringing uses," notably "time-shifting" [1]. While fundamentally a fair use decision, it established the consumer hardware ecosystem required for home video playback. This directly triggered the boom of the video rental industry, which relied on the "first sale doctrine" (17 U.S.C. § 109) to buy studio-produced VHS tapes and rent them out without ongoing copyright holder permission [3].

In *Kirtsaeng*, the issue was whether the first sale doctrine applied to foreign-made copyrighted goods. A student imported and resold lower-priced textbooks legally manufactured abroad by an American publisher [2]. The Court ruled for Kirtsaeng, determining that the first sale doctrine applies to copies of works lawfully made anywhere internationally, thereby preventing copyright owners from using geographic boundaries to segment secondary markets [4]. 

Together, these legal principles protected the physical media rental economy. *Sony* guaranteed the consumer hardware access necessary for brick-and-mortar stores like Blockbuster to build massive VHS rental businesses shielded by the first sale doctrine [3]. Decades later, *Kirtsaeng* safeguarded modern iterations like Netflix’s DVD-by-mail and Redbox kiosks. It ensured that rental distributors could legally source physical discs (DVDs and Blu-rays) from global markets, preventing movie studios from stifling domestic resale and rental markets through regional copyright restrictions or international price discrimination [5].

## References
[1] Oyez. "Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc." IIT Chicago-Kent College of Law. https://www.oyez.org/cases/1982/81-1687
[2] Oyez. "Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc." IIT Chicago-Kent College of Law. https://www.oyez.org/cases/2012/11-697
[3] American Library Association. "First Sale Doctrine." Copyright & Fair Use. https://www.ala.org/advocacy/copyright/firstsale
[4] Justia. "Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)." U.S. Supreme Court Center. https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/568/519/
[5] Electronic Frontier Foundation. "Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons." EFF Cases. https://www.eff.org/cases/kirtsaeng-v-john-wiley-sons
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified *Sony Corp. v. Universal City Studios* (1984) and *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons* (2013), accurately describing the Betamax/VCR dispute and the foreign-made textbook resale issue. Both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both explain the core legal issues, the Court’s rulings, and the connection to the first sale doctrine and the video rental industry (Blockbuster, Netflix, Redbox). Neither mentions the specific 5–4 or 6–3 vote splits, but both adequately describe the holdings and reasoning. Agent B provides slightly richer doctrinal framing (explicit reference to 17 U.S.C. § 109, geographic market segmentation, and price discrimination), and more clearly distinguishes Sony as primarily a fair use/contributory liability case that enabled the hardware ecosystem underpinning rentals.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Both are well structured and readable, but Agent B offers greater citation density and more contextual explanation about global sourcing and regional copyright restrictions. This makes B more comprehensive and closer to a high-end search engine response. Since both are factually strong and complete, B wins on depth and user utility rather than correcting any major flaw in A.

Revolution in Resale of Digital Books and Music
rounds.question: The Supreme Court’s endorsement of the “first sale” doctrine in the 1980s helped enable the rise of video rental shops by protecting the right to rent or resell lawfully purchased tapes and discs. Decades later, several technology companies and a start-up attempted to apply similar resale principles to purely digital media, proposing systems that would allow consumers to transfer or resell legally acquired e-books or music files without creating duplicate copies. Identify the two major technology companies that sought patents for digital resale marketplaces, as well as the start-up that was sued by a major record label over its secondary market for digital songs. For each entity, describe (1) how its proposed or implemented system would prevent multiple copies from existing simultaneously, and (2) the specific legal or industry reaction it faced. Answer length: 250-350 words.
checklist_width: Amazon – description of its patented digital marketplace/exchange system
Amazon – explanation that transferred items would be deleted from the seller’s account/data store to prevent duplicate copies
Apple – description of its patent application for transferring digital media (e-books, music, movies, software)
Apple – explanation that the system would allow only one user to possess a copy at a time via file transfer rather than reproduction
ReDigi – identification as a start-up enabling resale of iTunes songs
ReDigi – description of Capitol Records lawsuit and the copyright infringement claim
ReDigi – explanation of its technology designed to ensure only one copy existed at any time
checklist_depth: Amazon – major technology company seeking a patent for a digital resale marketplace in the context of first-sale style rights for digital goods
Apple – major technology company filing a patent application for a digital secondary market for media files
ReDigi – start-up sued by Capitol Records for facilitating resale of digital music files
rounds.rationale: The question uses the historical logic of the first-sale doctrine that enabled video rental shops (deep reasoning anchor) to filter for modern digital equivalents attempting to recreate resale rights. It masks the entities by describing their function and legal context rather than naming them. It requires wide aggregation because the respondent must identify three separate entities and compile distinct technical mechanisms and legal reactions for each, drawing from multiple sources rather than a single fact.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Technology | Imagining a Swap Meet for E-Books and Music https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/08/technology/revolution-in-the-resale-of-digital-books-and-music.html Advertisement C
rounds.answer_a:
Two large technology companies that pursued patents for digital resale marketplaces were **Amazon** and **Apple**, and the start-up was **ReDigi**.

**Amazon.**  
(1) Amazon’s 2013 U.S. patent application for a “secondary market for digital objects” described a system in which a user’s e-book, music file, or other digital item would be moved from the seller’s personal “data store” to the buyer’s, with Amazon’s servers enforcing that only one active copy existed at a time. The system envisioned “transfer” rather than duplication, with deletion or deactivation of the seller’s access upon resale.[1]  
(2) The patent drew intense commentary from copyright lawyers and publishers, who warned it could collide with the first sale doctrine’s limits in the digital realm, where copies are made in transmission. No full-scale consumer marketplace was launched, in part because of legal uncertainty and resistance from rightsholders.

**Apple.**  
(1) Apple obtained a 2013 U.S. patent for a system allowing users to resell or lend digital content in their iTunes library. The scheme relied on a central server to track ownership and would revoke or limit the seller’s access once a resale occurred, again aiming to mimic “transfer” of a single authorized copy.[2]  
(2) The industry reaction was wary; labels and publishers were already licensing Apple under tightly controlled terms. Apple never rolled out a broad public digital-resale feature, likely due to contract and copyright risks.

**ReDigi.**  
(1) ReDigi offered a marketplace for “used” iTunes music. Its software claimed to transfer a file “bit by bit” from a user’s computer to ReDigi’s cloud while deleting the original, so that only one copy existed and could later be resold.[3]  
(2) Capitol Records sued, and the Second Circuit held that ReDigi’s process made unauthorized reproductions; digital first sale did not excuse those new copies. The ruling effectively shut down ReDigi’s model and chilled similar digital-resale efforts.[3]

## References
[1] U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595 (Amazon “secondary market for digital objects”), 2013.  
[2] U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595 B2 / related Apple patent reports on digital resale (2013); coverage in The Verge, 2013.  
[3] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 910 F.3d 649 (2d Cir. 2018); E.D.N.Y. opinion 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (2013).
rounds.answer_b:
The two major technology companies that sought patents for digital resale marketplaces are Amazon and Apple, while the start-up sued over its secondary market for digital media was ReDigi [1][2][3].

**ReDigi** established a platform allowing users to resell legally purchased digital music files. To prevent unauthorized duplication, ReDigi utilized proprietary "forward-and-delete" software called MediaManager. This system migrated the digital file bit-by-bit from the seller’s local drive to a cloud server, theoretically deleting the original simultaneously so that two copies never existed at once [1]. Despite this technical safeguard, Capitol Records sued ReDigi for copyright infringement. In a landmark ruling, the court held that the digital "first sale" doctrine did not apply. The court reasoned that transferring a file over the internet necessarily creates a new, unauthorized digital reproduction on a new server, violating the rightsholder’s exclusive reproduction rights [1][4].

**Amazon** received a patent in 2013 for a "Secondary Market for Digital Objects" (such as e-books, audio, and video) [2]. Its proposed system managed digital lockers by transferring a file to the buyer's locker while simultaneously deleting or revoking access to the file in the seller’s streaming account [2]. Although the patent caused widespread concern among authors and publishers fearing the cannibalization of primary digital sales, Amazon never launched a widespread secondary marketplace, largely chilled by the hostile legal climate established by the ReDigi dispute [4].

**Apple** filed a patent application around the same time for a system managing the transfer of digital items within its ecosystem. Apple’s method relied on transferring cryptographic keys or digital rights management (DRM) tokens; once an item was sold, the system removed the digital token from the seller’s device, rendering their local file unplayable, while providing a new token to the buyer [3]. Like Amazon, Apple faced severe skepticism from copyright holders who feared a secondhand market would devastate digital profits, and Apple has similarly refrained from launching a commercial digital resale marketplace [3][5].

## References
[1] Electronic Frontier Foundation. "Capitol Records v. ReDigi." https://www.eff.org/cases/capitol-records-v-redigi
[2] United States Patent and Trademark Office. "Secondary Market for Digital Objects." U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595, issued January 29, 2013.
[3] Patently Apple. "Apple Inventions Resurrect the Subject of a Digital Resale Market." https://www.patentlyapple.com/patently-apple/2013/03/apple-inventions-resurrect-the-subject-of-a-digital-resale-market.html
[4] The New York Times. "A Setback for Resellers of Digital Products." https://www.nytimes.com/2013/04/02/business/media/redigi-loses-suit-over-reselling-of-digital-music.html
[5] WIRED. "Amazon and Apple Patent Used-Digital-Goods Stores." https://www.wired.com/2013/03/amazon-and-apple-patents/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Amazon and Apple as the major technology companies pursuing digital resale patents and ReDigi as the start-up sued by Capitol Records. Both therefore pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both answers address all required sub-points: (1) how each system attempted to ensure only one copy existed at a time, and (2) the legal or industry reaction each faced. Agent A clearly explains Amazon’s server-enforced transfer and deletion model, Apple’s ownership-tracking transfer system, and ReDigi’s bit-by-bit forward-and-delete method, along with the Second Circuit’s rejection of the digital first-sale defense. Agent B does the same but adds slightly more technical specificity for Apple (e.g., DRM token transfer) and more explicit discussion of the court’s reproduction-right reasoning in ReDigi. Agent A has a minor patent citation inconsistency (repeating Amazon’s patent number in the Apple reference), though the substantive description remains accurate.

Third, User Experience & Presentation: Both are well-structured and scannable, using bold headers and clear organization. However, Agent B provides greater citation density, slightly richer legal context, and clearer articulation of the technological mechanisms (especially for Apple and ReDigi). This gives B a marginal edge in perceived authority and completeness.

Conclusion: Since both are factually strong and complete, but Agent B offers somewhat deeper technical detail and stronger contextual grounding, B wins on overall user experience. No major checklist failures for either, so the loser_failure_type is NONE.
